caveats: a: OEWS covers all dairy manufacturing, including cheese, butter, and dry dairy products, not fluid milk alone.
a: Physical automation is counted only where AI changes current tasks, not as a generic capital-equipment opportunity.
rho: Reported smart-manufacturing gains are self-reported, cross-industry, and not AI-specific.
rho: PMO constraints limit autonomous control changes more than back-office and decision-support workflows.
e: Public co-packer examples establish the model but do not measure industry prevalence.
e: The frozen firm count is an EBITDA-band estimate and may include cooperatives, mixed-product plants, or branded-only firms.
s5: Cross-industry owner intentions are not completed transfers.
s5: Consolidation can create transactions while simultaneously shrinking the independent target population.
q: Contract terms vary among regional retail, school, institutional, and branded-beverage customers.
q: No public evidence directly measures retained AI benefit for fluid-milk co-packers.
d5: Per-capita beverage-milk decline is not the same as total eligible co-manufacturing volume.
d5: The NAICS definition includes fluid dairy substitutes, while some long-shelf-life products are classified elsewhere.
o: Operator necessity is high, but the relevant independent operator can be displaced by a captive customer plant.
o: Quantity eliminated by beverage substitution belongs in d5 and is not deducted again here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Retailers, institutions, foodservice operators, and beverage brands contract for repeated production and packaging runs, generally under per-gallon, per-unit, tolling, or supply agreements with raw-material, packaging, freight, and commodity-price adjustment mechanisms.
lens.excluded_activities: Own-brand-only dairies, captive retailer plants, dairy farms, distributors without processing responsibility, dry or ultra-high-temperature milk operations classified in 311514, frozen-dessert plants, non-control financing vehicles, and facilities primarily classified outside 311511.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market dairy and beverage processors providing recurring private-label or contract pasteurization, homogenization, blending, filling, packaging, cold-chain, quality-assurance, and related manufacturing services for fluid milk, cream, sour cream, and fluid dairy substitutes classified in 311511.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupation employment and wages for broader NAICS 311500 rather than task-level AI exposure in 311511 contract processors. The estimate maps higher exposure to demand and production planning, routing, order entry, procurement, quality records, regulatory documentation, forecasting, maintenance analysis, customer service, and inspection support while assigning low exposure to physical processing, sanitation, maintenance execution, driving, filling, and material handling. Four-digit aggregation and all-size coverage can bias either direction.
caveats: OEWS covers all dairy manufacturing, including cheese, butter, and dry dairy products, not fluid milk alone.
Physical automation is counted only where AI changes current tasks, not as a generic capital-equipment opportunity.
a.change_evidence: Collect payroll-weighted task diaries and system logs from 10-15 independent fluid-milk/private-label processors, separating already automated PLC, filling, and route systems from current human work by role.
a.rationale: In dairy manufacturing, production occupations represent 47.64% of employment, transportation and material moving 18.61%, maintenance 7.60%, and office support 6.77%. AI can materially assist the information-intensive portion of plant and route operations, but most payroll remains tied to embodied cold-chain and processing work.

d5.bridge: USDA route-disposition and per-capita consumption series measure U.S. fluid beverage milk sales rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for the eligible contract-processing basket, which also includes cream, sour cream, flavored products, and dairy substitutes. The five-year estimate extrapolates the persistent beverage-milk decline but widens upward for population, school and institutional channels, product innovation, and substitutes. Historical per-capita decline may overstate contraction for diversified co-packers.
caveats: Per-capita beverage-milk decline is not the same as total eligible co-manufacturing volume.
The NAICS definition includes fluid dairy substitutes, while some long-shelf-life products are classified elsewhere.
d5.change_evidence: Use USDA annual fluid-sales quantities, Circana/Nielsen unit volumes, school and institutional procurement, cream and sour-cream volumes, dairy-alternative volumes, and eligible-processor shipments, all segmented and adjusted for population, price, package size, and quality.
d5.rationale: USDA reports fluid milk consumption declined every decade for more than 70 years and fell 20.7% from 2010 to 2019; the agency's 2014-2024 chart shows the decline continuing. An AP report cited a 22% per-capita decline from 2011 to 2021. Cream, cultured products, flavored milk, dairy alternatives, population growth, and private-label share shifts temper but do not erase the headwind.

caveats: Public co-packer examples establish the model but do not measure industry prevalence.
The frozen firm count is an EBITDA-band estimate and may include cooperatives, mixed-product plants, or branded-only firms.
e.change_evidence: Map all 79 dataset-estimated LMM firms through IMS/plant lists, retailer labels, customer contracts, plant capability pages, and management interviews; verify the revenue share from recurring external private-label and co-manufacturing work.
e.rationale: Many fluid-milk processors sell their own regional brands or are captive cooperative and retailer plants, which fail the outsourced-service lens. Private-label and co-packing are nonetheless established for conventional milk, cream, cultured products, dairy alternatives, and customized beverages. Mixed plants qualify only when recurring external contract production is operationally material and transferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311511 ($4,340,077,217) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311511 ($49,661,946,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $870,377 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311511); class <5: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,059,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $161,533 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,934,080 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $447,447 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,161,864 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,244,684 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $23,541,895 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,590,139 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $173,227,976 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $26,417,266 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $930,199,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $141,855,445 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 79

o.bridge: FDA's PMO and milk-safety materials demonstrate that physical processors remain accountable for pasteurization, equipment, sanitation, testing, cooling, and packaging, but do not measure the year-five share requiring an independent lens operator. The estimate assigns high operator necessity to surviving output and discounts for retailer vertical integration and customer insourcing. Regulation makes software-only replacement unlikely but does not protect outsourcing.
caveats: Operator necessity is high, but the relevant independent operator can be displaced by a captive customer plant.
Quantity eliminated by beverage substitution belongs in d5 and is not deducted again here.
o.change_evidence: Interview retailer, institutional, and beverage-brand procurement teams about five-year insourcing, plant construction, supplier switching, regulatory responsibility, alternative formats, and whether any current processing quantity could be self-served without a specialized plant operator.
o.rationale: Fluid products require receipt, testing, separation or standardization, pasteurization, homogenization, cooling, filling, sanitation, and cold-chain or aseptic handling. The PMO specifies chemical, physical, bacteriological, temperature, sanitation, equipment, and processing standards. Walmart's $350 million milk plant illustrates that customers may self-supply, but even insourced volume still requires a physical accountable processor rather than software alone.

caveats: Contract terms vary among regional retail, school, institutional, and branded-beverage customers.
No public evidence directly measures retained AI benefit for fluid-milk co-packers.
q.change_evidence: Review customer contracts and renewal histories for conversion-fee formulas, raw-milk and packaging pass-through, freight, productivity clauses, audit rights, service penalties, term, customer concentration, and realized post-automation margins.
q.rationale: Private-label fluid milk is a price-sensitive, frequently negotiated product with visible commodity inputs, packaging, freight, and labor economics. Fixed conversion fees can preserve savings between resets, and gains in yield, spoilage, uptime, service, route density, and quality may be less transparent. Large retailers and institutions can reclaim direct-labor benefits through formula pricing, rebids, audits, or insourcing.

rho.bridge: Deloitte measures smart-manufacturing adoption and outcomes across industries and company sizes, not five-year implementation of AI-exposed tasks in LMM fluid-milk plants. Its productivity results anchor technical feasibility; the estimate is reduced for legacy controls, perishability, data integration, cybersecurity, customer qualification, PMO requirements, sanitation, and thin margins. The survey likely biases implementation upward because it includes large manufacturers and non-AI technologies.
caveats: Reported smart-manufacturing gains are self-reported, cross-industry, and not AI-specific.
PMO constraints limit autonomous control changes more than back-office and decision-support workflows.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain five-year deployment and realized-hour data for dairy MES/ERP, route optimization, predictive maintenance, quality-documentation, laboratory review, vision inspection, order automation, and scheduling projects at LMM processors, including failed pilots and regulatory observations.
rho.rationale: Deloitte respondents reported 7%-20% employee-productivity improvement and 10%-15% unlocked capacity after smart-manufacturing implementation, and 46% ranked process automation among their top two priorities. Fluid-milk facilities can adopt planning, route, quality, and maintenance tools, but regulated time-temperature controls, equipment seals, testing, cold-chain continuity, and food-safety accountability require validated, human-supervised deployment.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among eligible 311511 firms. The 22% employer intention rate anchors the estimate and is modestly reduced for specialized facilities, cooperatives, customer concentration, and a consolidating buyer set; a recent multi-generation dairy acquisition confirms transfer feasibility. Intentions likely overstate completions, while unplanned and confidential deals offset some bias.
caveats: Cross-industry owner intentions are not completed transfers.
Consolidation can create transactions while simultaneously shrinking the independent target population.
s5.change_evidence: Construct a five-year ownership-transition cohort for eligible fluid processors using IMS plant histories, PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, state corporate records, dairy lenders, cooperatives, and industry brokers, excluding closures, minority investments, and internal reorganizations.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. Prairie Farms' 2024 acquisition of fourth-generation SmithFoods and its Ohio dairy facilities demonstrates a qualifying operating-business transfer, but one deal provides no denominator-based rate.

principal_driver: Regulatory and cold-chain requirements make surviving fluid-product volume inseparable from an accountable physical processor.
principal_weakness: Secular beverage-milk decline, low labor intensity, customer bargaining, and retailer insourcing limit both opportunity size and retention.

sources.supports: Defines 311511 as manufacturing processed milk products such as pasteurized milk, cream, and sour cream, and fluid milk substitutes from soybeans and other nondairy substances.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 311511 Fluid Milk Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports dairy-manufacturing employment and wages, including 47.64% production, 18.61% transportation/material moving, 7.60% maintenance, and 6.77% office-support employment.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311500

sources.supports: A 600-respondent manufacturing survey reporting 7%-20% employee-productivity improvement, 10%-15% unlocked capacity, and process automation as a top-two priority for 46% of respondents.
sources.title: 2025 Smart Manufacturing and Operations Survey

sources.supports: Specifies chemical, physical, bacteriological, temperature, sanitation, equipment, pasteurization, aseptic-processing, packaging, and related standards for Grade A milk and milk products.
sources.title: Grade A Pasteurized Milk Ordinance, 2023 Revision

sources.supports: Explains PMO coverage of heating, cooling, transport, storage, processing and packaging equipment, worker safety, laboratory testing, and plant traceability codes.
sources.title: Keeping Your Milk Safe From the Grass to the Glass

sources.supports: Documents private-label and co-packing for pasteurized and homogenized fluid milk, dairy products, and nondairy items.
sources.title: Scott Brothers Dairy Private Label and Co-Packing Capabilities

sources.supports: Documents private-label beverage co-manufacturing, formulation, scale-up, shelf-life and analytical testing, ESL processing, and aseptic milk capabilities.
sources.title: Byrne Dairy Private Label and Beverage Co-Manufacturing Capabilities

sources.supports: Reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years in a fall 2024 survey of 1,264 U.S. owners.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports Prairie Farms' October 2024 acquisition of fourth-generation SmithFoods and its Ohio dairy facilities producing milk, cultured products, custom blends, and plant-based beverages.
sources.title: Prairie Farms Acquires Dairy Manufacturer SmithFoods

sources.supports: Reports more than 70 years of per-capita decline, including a 20.7% fall from 2010 to 2019, and documents generational changes, school-channel shares, and limited explanatory power of plant alternatives.
sources.title: Fluid Milk Consumption Continues Downward Trend, Proving Difficult to Reverse

sources.supports: Reports Walmart's planned captive milk-processing plant, the retailer's move from external dairy suppliers toward production control, and a USDA-cited 22% per-capita milk decline from 2011 to 2021.
sources.title: Walmart Will Build a $350 Million Milk Plant in South Georgia

title: Fluid Milk Manufacturing
